trans-unit.source: Geodata management
trans-unit.target: Geodata management

trans-unit.source: In this chapter, we will have a closer look at how to store geodata on your computer to work in QGIS or other GIS applications. Since vector data is the primary geodata type you will work with at the beginning of your GIS career, we will focus on vector geodata. We will learn how to set up a coherent folder structure for your GIS-data and projects and how to name geodata correctly.
trans-unit.target: In this chapter, we will have a closer look at how to store geodata on your computer to work in QGIS or other GIS applications. Since vector data is the primary geodata type you will work with at the beginning of your GIS career, we will focus on vector geodata. We will learn how to set up a coherent folder structure for your GIS-data and projects and how to name geodata correctly.

trans-unit.source: Fundamentals of Geodata Management
trans-unit.target: Fundamentals of Geodata Management

trans-unit.source: Working with geodata is not like working with data in programs such as Microsoft Excel or Word. Whenever you load an image in a Word file, the file will contain the image. If you delete the image on your computer, the Word file will still contain a copy of the image.
trans-unit.target: Working with geodata is not like working with data in programs such as Microsoft Excel or Word. Whenever you load an image in a Word file, the file will contain the image. If you delete the image on your computer, the Word file will still contain a copy of the image.

trans-unit.source: This is not the case in QGIS! When you load geodata into QGIS, the system only establishes a link to the location where the data is stored on your computer. This means your QGIS project __does not contain__ the geodata, it only links to it. If you load data in your QGIS project and you change the location of the data or delete it, the data is no longer available in your QGIS project and you will get an error when you open it.
trans-unit.target: This is not the case in QGIS! When you load geodata into QGIS, the system only establishes a link to the location where the data is stored on your computer. This means your QGIS project __does not contain__ the geodata, it only links to it. If you load data in your QGIS project and you change the location of the data or delete it, the data is no longer available in your QGIS project and you will get an error when you open it.

trans-unit.source: Because you are working directly with the source data, rather than a copy, whenever you edit data in QGIS the changes replace the source data and can not be reversed. If you plan to make changes to your data, you should make a copy of it first so that you always have a 'clean' copy you can go back to.
trans-unit.target: Because you are working directly with the source data, rather than a copy, whenever you edit data in QGIS the changes replace the source data and can not be reversed. If you plan to make changes to your data, you should make a copy of it first so that you always have a 'clean' copy you can go back to.

trans-unit.source: Standard folder structure
trans-unit.target: Standard folder structure

trans-unit.source: The single most important geodata management practice is to use a standardised folder structure that contains all parts of the QGIS project.
trans-unit.target: The single most important geodata management practice is to use a standardised folder structure that contains all parts of the QGIS project.

trans-unit.source: The paths from a QGIS project to the geodata are by default relative. This means when the data and the project are in a fixed folder structure, you can move the whole structure without impacting the QGIS project or the paths to the data. The version of a standardized folder structure that is used for all exercises in this training is explained below. A template of the folder structure can be downloaded [__here__](https://nexus.heigit.org/repository/gis-training-resource-center/Templates/GIS_project_folder_template.zip).
trans-unit.target: The paths from a QGIS project to the geodata are by default relative. This means when the data and the project are in a fixed folder structure, you can move the whole structure without impacting the QGIS project or the paths to the data. The version of a standardized folder structure that is used for all exercises in this training is explained below. A template of the folder structure can be downloaded [__here__](https://nexus.heigit.org/repository/gis-training-resource-center/Templates/GIS_project_folder_template.zip).

trans-unit.source: A standard folder structure has two principal advantages:
trans-unit.target: A standard folder structure has two principal advantages:

trans-unit.source: If we share the whole project folder, we can expect the project to run without problems on a different computer
trans-unit.target: If we share the whole project folder, we can expect the project to run without problems on a different computer

trans-unit.source: The folder structure supports the proper organisation of project data and helps ensure the QGIS project will work as intended
trans-unit.target: The folder structure supports the proper organisation of project data and helps ensure the QGIS project will work as intended

trans-unit.source: Standard folder structure. Source: HeiGIT :::
trans-unit.target: Standard folder structure. Source: HeiGIT :::

trans-unit.source: Naming Geodata
trans-unit.target: Naming Geodata

trans-unit.source: Naming your data correctly ensures that you can identify the layers and your computer does not run into any issues when working with your data files. The name of your files themselves need to be clear, meaning that you or others can identify what the data shows, where the data comes from, and to what time it refers. In QGIS, you should name your layers so you can identify the content, as well as what you have done with the layer. For example, if you have clipped a street layer of New York, do not name the layer "clipped", give it a name such as "streets_NYC_clipped".
trans-unit.target: Naming your data correctly ensures that you can identify the layers and your computer does not run into any issues when working with your data files. The name of your files themselves need to be clear, meaning that you or others can identify what the data shows, where the data comes from, and to what time it refers. In QGIS, you should name your layers so you can identify the content, as well as what you have done with the layer. For example, if you have clipped a street layer of New York, do not name the layer "clipped", give it a name such as "streets_NYC_clipped".

trans-unit.source: There are some basic principles when it comes to naming geodata that you produce or manipulate:
trans-unit.target: There are some basic principles when it comes to naming geodata that you produce or manipulate:

trans-unit.source: Do not use special characters like `!`,`?`, `/` or `-`.
trans-unit.target: Do not use special characters like `!`,`?`, `/` or `-`.

trans-unit.source: Do not use blank spaces, use underscores `_`
trans-unit.target: Do not use blank spaces, use underscores `_`

trans-unit.source: Give layers meaningful names so that you can understand what they represent, even if they are a temporary/intermediate step in a workflow.
trans-unit.target: Give layers meaningful names so that you can understand what they represent, even if they are a temporary/intermediate step in a workflow.

trans-unit.source: Below you can see an example of a workflow to process an admin boundary dataset (`adm0`). The purpose of the intermediate steps is not clear because the layer names are not meaningful.
trans-unit.target: Below you can see an example of a workflow to process an admin boundary dataset (`adm0`). The purpose of the intermediate steps is not clear because the layer names are not meaningful.

trans-unit.source: A good naming system for layers is shown below. In this workflow, it is clear what processing was performed at each step (reproject, clip layer, join with another layer, final result). In this way, other people can understand what purpose different layers serve and whether they are needed in the final project.
trans-unit.target: A good naming system for layers is shown below. In this workflow, it is clear what processing was performed at each step (reproject, clip layer, join with another layer, final result). In this way, other people can understand what purpose different layers serve and whether they are needed in the final project.

trans-unit.source: Documentation
trans-unit.target: Documentation

trans-unit.source: Documentation is an important step when working with geodata or performing analyses. It ensures clarity, reproducibility, and enables collaboration. Spatial data analysis often involves complex processes, data cleaning, data transformations, and specific data sources. Without proper documentation, it becomes difficult for yourself and others to understand, replicate, or build upon your work. Documentation helps organise the purpose, the methods or tools, the data inputs and outputs, as well as the assumptions and limitations. In general, good documentation allows GIS-practitioners to reproduce the analysis steps to get the exact same result. In collaborative work, good documentation serves as a roadmap and is essential when working on GIS-projects. In humanitarian work, and in decision-making processes, good documentation is essential as it helps inform decision making which enables humanitarian teams to allocate resources.
trans-unit.target: Documentation is an important step when working with geodata or performing analyses. It ensures clarity, reproducibility, and enables collaboration. Spatial data analysis often involves complex processes, data cleaning, data transformations, and specific data sources. Without proper documentation, it becomes difficult for yourself and others to understand, replicate, or build upon your work. Documentation helps organise the purpose, the methods or tools, the data inputs and outputs, as well as the assumptions and limitations. In general, good documentation allows GIS-practitioners to reproduce the analysis steps to get the exact same result. In collaborative work, good documentation serves as a roadmap and is essential when working on GIS-projects. In humanitarian work, and in decision-making processes, good documentation is essential as it helps inform decision making which enables humanitarian teams to allocate resources.

trans-unit.source: You can document your projects using markdown editors or just simply creating a word document. Make sure it is saved in the documentation subfolder in your project folder. There are no set rules to write a documentation, however, adhering to a logical structure can help writing and reading your documentation. It is also advisable to write the documentation while you are performing the analysis. QGIS offers a lot of options and settings while performing an analysis, and it can be easy to forget the parameters you have used for an analysis step.
trans-unit.target: You can document your projects using markdown editors or just simply creating a word document. Make sure it is saved in the documentation subfolder in your project folder. There are no set rules to write a documentation, however, adhering to a logical structure can help writing and reading your documentation. It is also advisable to write the documentation while you are performing the analysis. QGIS offers a lot of options and settings while performing an analysis, and it can be easy to forget the parameters you have used for an analysis step.

trans-unit.source: __Project overview:__
trans-unit.target: __Project overview:__

trans-unit.source: Add the title and purpose of the project.
trans-unit.target: Add the title and purpose of the project.

trans-unit.source: Add a brief summary of the analysis.
trans-unit.target: Add a brief summary of the analysis.

trans-unit.source: __Data sources:__
trans-unit.target: __Data sources:__

trans-unit.source: List of all the input datasets (with links if possible)
trans-unit.target: List of all the input datasets (with links if possible)

trans-unit.source: Coordinate system used
trans-unit.target: Coordinate system used

trans-unit.source: Notes on data quality or limitations
trans-unit.target: Notes on data quality or limitations

trans-unit.source: __Software & Tools:__
trans-unit.target: __Software & Tools:__

trans-unit.source: QGIS version
trans-unit.target: QGIS version

trans-unit.source: Plugins used
trans-unit.target: Plugins used

trans-unit.source: __Workflow & Methodology:__
trans-unit.target: __Workflow & Methodology:__

trans-unit.source: Step-by-step process with screenshots or diagrams where helpful
trans-unit.target: Step-by-step process with screenshots or diagrams where helpful

trans-unit.source: Describe each processing step (these can be in bullet points)
trans-unit.target: Describe each processing step (these can be in bullet points)

trans-unit.source: Mention key parameters or decisions made
trans-unit.target: Mention key parameters or decisions made

trans-unit.source: Make sure yourself and other persons can understand and replicate the analysis steps. Good documentation turns your QGIS project from a black box into a transparent, shareable, and professional piece of work.
trans-unit.target: Make sure yourself and other persons can understand and replicate the analysis steps. Good documentation turns your QGIS project from a black box into a transparent, shareable, and professional piece of work.

trans-unit.source: Self-Assessment Questions
trans-unit.target: Self-Assessment Questions

trans-unit.source: ::::{admonition} Test your knowledge :class: note
trans-unit.target: ::::{admonition} Test your knowledge :class: note

trans-unit.source: Take a moment to test what you've learned in this chapter by answering the questions below:
trans-unit.target: Take a moment to test what you've learned in this chapter by answering the questions below:

trans-unit.source: __Where are the layers loaded into a QGIS project saved? What implications does this have when moving or renaming files?__
trans-unit.target: __Where are the layers loaded into a QGIS project saved? What implications does this have when moving or renaming files?__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: Layers loaded into a QGIS project are not embedded into the .qgz project file — they are linked via file paths to the original data sources.
trans-unit.target: Layers loaded into a QGIS project are not embedded into the .qgz project file — they are linked via file paths to the original data sources.

trans-unit.source: This means that if you move, rename, or delete a source file after adding it to the project, QGIS will not be able to find it, and the layer will appear broken or missing.
trans-unit.target: This means that if you move, rename, or delete a source file after adding it to the project, QGIS will not be able to find it, and the layer will appear broken or missing.

trans-unit.source: To avoid this, maintain a consistent folder structure and avoid changing file locations once the project is set up — or update the file paths if you do. :::
trans-unit.target: To avoid this, maintain a consistent folder structure and avoid changing file locations once the project is set up — or update the file paths if you do. :::

trans-unit.source: __When editing geodata in QGIS, why is it recommended to make a copy first?__
trans-unit.target: __When editing geodata in QGIS, why is it recommended to make a copy first?__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: Because QGIS edits the original data file directly, there is no built-in undo or automatic backup once changes are saved.
trans-unit.target: Because QGIS edits the original data file directly, there is no built-in undo or automatic backup once changes are saved.

trans-unit.source: If you make a mistake during editing, you can’t easily revert it.
trans-unit.target: If you make a mistake during editing, you can’t easily revert it.

trans-unit.source: Creating a copy of the dataset before editing preserves the original data and gives you a fallback in case anything goes wrong. :::
trans-unit.target: Creating a copy of the dataset before editing preserves the original data and gives you a fallback in case anything goes wrong. :::

trans-unit.source: __What are two principal advantages of using a standardized folder structure for GIS projects and data?__
trans-unit.target: __What are two principal advantages of using a standardized folder structure for GIS projects and data?__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: __Portability:__ If the entire project folder (including data subfolders) is moved to a new computer or drive, the QGIS project can still find its data as long as relative paths are used.
trans-unit.target: __Portability:__ If the entire project folder (including data subfolders) is moved to a new computer or drive, the QGIS project can still find its data as long as relative paths are used.

trans-unit.source: __Organization & consistency__: A clear, consistent structure helps avoid misplaced files, makes the project easier to navigate, and ensures others can understand and work with your project more easily. :::
trans-unit.target: __Organization & consistency__: A clear, consistent structure helps avoid misplaced files, makes the project easier to navigate, and ensures others can understand and work with your project more easily. :::

trans-unit.source: __Describe at least three good practices for naming geodata / layers.__
trans-unit.target: __Describe at least three good practices for naming geodata / layers.__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: Avoid special characters and spaces in file names — use underscores `_` instead (e.g., `land_cover_2020.shp`).
trans-unit.target: Avoid special characters and spaces in file names — use underscores `_` instead (e.g., `land_cover_2020.shp`).

trans-unit.source: Use descriptive names that indicate the content of the layer (e.g., `population_density_europe` instead of just `data1`).
trans-unit.target: Use descriptive names that indicate the content of the layer (e.g., `population_density_europe` instead of just `data1`).

trans-unit.source: Indicate processing steps in the name if the layer has been modified (e.g., `rivers_clipped`, `buildings_buffered_500m`) to make your workflow transparent and reproducible. :::
trans-unit.target: Indicate processing steps in the name if the layer has been modified (e.g., `rivers_clipped`, `buildings_buffered_500m`) to make your workflow transparent and reproducible. :::

trans-unit.source: __If you move the entire project folder (with its subfolders and data) to a different computer, under what conditions will the QGIS project still work without broken paths?__
trans-unit.target: __If you move the entire project folder (with its subfolders and data) to a different computer, under what conditions will the QGIS project still work without broken paths?__

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Answer
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Answer

trans-unit.source: The project will still work if:
trans-unit.target: The project will still work if:

trans-unit.source: all of the files used in the project __are contained inside the project folder__.
trans-unit.target: all of the files used in the project __are contained inside the project folder__.

trans-unit.source: You do not use plugins or resources that are not available on the different computer
trans-unit.target: You do not use plugins or resources that are not available on the different computer

trans-unit.source: If any files were loaded using absolute paths (e.g., from a location outside the project folder), those paths will likely break after moving to a new machine. :::
trans-unit.target: If any files were loaded using absolute paths (e.g., from a location outside the project folder), those paths will likely break after moving to a new machine. :::
